title: Easter

infobox.value: Celebrates the [resurrection of Jesus](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus")
[Church services](/wiki/Church_service "Church service"), festive family meals, [Easter egg](/wiki/Easter_egg "Easter egg") decoration, and gift-giving

[Septuagesima](/wiki/Septuagesima "Septuagesima"), [Sexagesima](/wiki/Sexagesima "Sexagesima"), [Quinquagesima](/wiki/Quinquagesima "Quinquagesima"), [Shrove Tuesday](/wiki/Shrove_Tuesday "Shrove Tuesday"), [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday"), [Clean Monday](/wiki/Clean_Monday "Clean Monday"), [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"), [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent"), [Friday of Sorrows](/wiki/Friday_of_Sorrows "Friday of Sorrows"), [Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday"), [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week"), [Maundy Thursday](/wiki/Maundy_Thursday "Maundy Thursday"), [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday"), and [Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday") which lead up to Easter; and [Divine Mercy Sunday](/wiki/Divine_Mercy_Sunday "Divine Mercy Sunday"), [Ascension](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus_Christ "Ascension of Jesus Christ"), [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost"), [Trinity Sunday](/wiki/Trinity_Sunday "Trinity Sunday"), [Corpus Christi](/wiki/Corpus_Christi_(feast) "Corpus Christi (feast)"), and [Feast of the Sacred Heart](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Sacred_Heart "Feast of the Sacred Heart"), which follow it.

([/pæsk/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) or **Pascha**[[b]](#cite_note-8) or **Resurrection Sunday**,[[c]](#cite_note-11) is a [Christian festival](/wiki/Christian_festival "Christian festival") and cultural [holiday](/wiki/Holiday "Holiday") commemorating the [resurrection of Jesus](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus") from the dead, described in the Bible's [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") as having occurred on the third day of [his burial](/wiki/Burial_of_Jesus "Burial of Jesus") following [his crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus") by the [Romans](/wiki/Roman_people "Roman people") at [Calvary](/wiki/Calvary "Calvary") c. 30 AD.[[9]](#cite_note-12)[[10]](#cite_note-13) It is the culmination of the [Passion of Jesus](/wiki/Passion_of_Jesus "Passion of Jesus"), preceded by [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent") (or [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent")), a 40-day period of [fasting](/wiki/Fasting "Fasting"), [prayer](/wiki/Christian_prayer "Christian prayer"), and [penance](/wiki/Penance "Penance").

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Commemoration of the resurrection of Jesus Christ

This article is about the Christian and cultural festival. For other uses, see [Easter (disambiguation)](/wiki/Easter_(disambiguation) "Easter (disambiguation)").

| Easter | |
| --- | --- |
| Having [destroyed the gates of Hell](/wiki/Harrowing_of_Hell "Harrowing of Hell"), [Jesus Christ](/wiki/Jesus_Christ "Jesus Christ") is depicted flanked by saints, raising [Adam](/wiki/Adam "Adam") and [Eve](/wiki/Eve "Eve") from their graves and trampling [death](/wiki/Death "Death"). [Fresco](/wiki/Fresco "Fresco") of the [resurrection](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus") at [The Chora](/wiki/The_Chora "The Chora") (c. 1315) | |

| Observances | [all-night vigil](/wiki/Easter_Vigil "Easter Vigil"), [sunrise service](/wiki/Sunrise_service "Sunrise service") |

| Related to | [Septuagesima](/wiki/Septuagesima "Septuagesima"), [Sexagesima](/wiki/Sexagesima "Sexagesima"), [Quinquagesima](/wiki/Quinquagesima "Quinquagesima"), [Shrove Tuesday](/wiki/Shrove_Tuesday "Shrove Tuesday"), [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday"), [Clean Monday](/wiki/Clean_Monday "Clean Monday"), [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"), [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent"), [Friday of Sorrows](/wiki/Friday_of_Sorrows "Friday of Sorrows"), [Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday"), [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week"), [Maundy Thursday](/wiki/Maundy_Thursday "Maundy Thursday"), [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday"), and [Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday") which lead up to Easter; and [Divine Mercy Sunday](/wiki/Divine_Mercy_Sunday "Divine Mercy Sunday"), [Ascension](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus_Christ "Ascension of Jesus Christ"), [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost"), [Trinity Sunday](/wiki/Trinity_Sunday "Trinity Sunday"), [Corpus Christi](/wiki/Corpus_Christi_(feast) "Corpus Christi (feast)"), and [Feast of the Sacred Heart](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Sacred_Heart "Feast of the Sacred Heart"), which follow it. |

**Easter**,[[a]](#cite_note-5) also called **Pasch** ([/pæsk/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) or **Pascha**[[b]](#cite_note-8) or **Resurrection Sunday**,[[c]](#cite_note-11) is a [Christian festival](/wiki/Christian_festival "Christian festival") and cultural [holiday](/wiki/Holiday "Holiday") commemorating the [resurrection of Jesus](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus") from the dead, described in the Bible's [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") as having occurred on the third day of [his burial](/wiki/Burial_of_Jesus "Burial of Jesus") following [his crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus") by the [Romans](/wiki/Roman_people "Roman people") at [Calvary](/wiki/Calvary "Calvary") c. 30 AD.[[9]](#cite_note-12)[[10]](#cite_note-13) It is the culmination of the [Passion of Jesus](/wiki/Passion_of_Jesus "Passion of Jesus"), preceded by [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent") (or [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent")), a 40-day period of [fasting](/wiki/Fasting "Fasting"), [prayer](/wiki/Christian_prayer "Christian prayer"), and [penance](/wiki/Penance "Penance").

Easter-observing [Christians](/wiki/Christians "Christians") commonly refer to the last week of Lent, before Easter, as [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week"), which in [Western Christianity](/wiki/Western_Christianity "Western Christianity") begins on [Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday") (marking the entrance of Jesus in Jerusalem), includes [Spy Wednesday](/wiki/Spy_Wednesday "Spy Wednesday") (on which the [betrayal of Jesus](/wiki/Betrayal_of_Jesus "Betrayal of Jesus") is mourned),[[11]](#cite_note-Cooper2013-14) and contains the days of the [Easter Triduum](/wiki/Easter_Triduum "Easter Triduum") including [Maundy Thursday](/wiki/Maundy_Thursday "Maundy Thursday"), commemorating the [Maundy](/wiki/Maundy_(foot_washing) "Maundy (foot washing)") and [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper"),[[12]](#cite_note-15)[[13]](#cite_note-16) as well as [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday"), commemorating the crucifixion and death of Jesus.[[14]](#cite_note-17) In [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity"), the same events are commemorated with the names of days all starting with "Holy" or "Holy and Great", and Easter itself might be called Great and Holy Pascha. In both Western and Eastern Christianity, [Eastertide](/wiki/Eastertide "Eastertide")—also known as the Easter or Paschal [season](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year")—begins on Easter Sunday and continues for seven weeks, concluding on the 50th day, [Pentecost Sunday](/wiki/Pentecost_Sunday "Pentecost Sunday"). However, in Eastern Christianity, the [leavetaking](/wiki/Leavetaking "Leavetaking") of the feast occurs on the 39th day, the eve of the [Feast of the Ascension](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Ascension "Feast of the Ascension").

Easter and its related holidays are [moveable feasts](/wiki/Moveable_feast "Moveable feast"), not falling on a fixed date; [its date](/wiki/Date_of_Easter "Date of Easter") is computed based on a [lunisolar calendar](/wiki/Lunisolar_calendar "Lunisolar calendar") (solar year plus Moon phase) similar to the [Hebrew calendar](/wiki/Hebrew_calendar "Hebrew calendar"), generating a number of [controversies](/wiki/Easter_controversy "Easter controversy"). The [First Council of Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea") (325) established common Paschal observance by all Christians on the first Sunday after the first [full moon](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_full_moon "Ecclesiastical full moon") on or after the vernal [equinox](/wiki/Equinox "Equinox").[[15]](#cite_note-oikoumene.org-18) Even if calculated on the basis of the [Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar"), the date of that full moon sometimes differs from that of the astronomical first full moon after the [March equinox](/wiki/March_equinox "March equinox").[[16]](#cite_note-19)

The English term may derive from the [Anglo-Saxon](/wiki/Anglo-Saxon "Anglo-Saxon") goddess name *[Ēostre](/wiki/%C4%92ostre "Ēostre")*; Easter is linked to the Jewish [Passover](/wiki/Passover "Passover") by its name ([Hebrew](/wiki/Hebrew_language "Hebrew language"): פֶּסַח, *pesach*; [Imperial Aramaic](/wiki/Imperial_Aramaic_language "Imperial Aramaic language"): פָּסחָא, romanized: *pascha*; whence the term *Pascha*), by its origin (according to the [synoptic Gospels](/wiki/Synoptic_Gospels "Synoptic Gospels"), both the crucifixion and the resurrection took place during the week of Passover)[[17]](#cite_note-20)[[18]](#cite_note-21) and by much of its symbolism, as well as by its position in the calendar. In most European languages, both the Christian Easter and the Jewish Passover are called by the same name; and in the older [English translations](/wiki/Bible_translations_into_English "Bible translations into English") of the Bible, as well, the term Easter was used to translate Passover.[[19]](#cite_note-22)

[Easter traditions](/wiki/Easter_traditions "Easter traditions") vary across the [Christian world](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom"), and include [sunrise services](/wiki/Sunrise_service "Sunrise service") or [late-night vigils](/wiki/Easter_Vigil "Easter Vigil"), exclamations and exchanges of [Paschal greetings](/wiki/Paschal_greeting "Paschal greeting"), [flowering the cross](/wiki/Flowering_the_cross "Flowering the cross"),[[20]](#cite_note-Whitehouse2022-23) the wearing of [Easter bonnets](/wiki/Easter_bonnet "Easter bonnet") by women, [clipping the church](/wiki/Clipping_the_church "Clipping the church"),[[21]](#cite_note-clipping_the_church-24) and the decoration and the communal breaking of [Easter eggs](/wiki/Easter_egg "Easter egg") (a symbol of the [empty tomb](/wiki/Empty_tomb "Empty tomb")).[[22]](#cite_note-Jordan2000-25)[[23]](#cite_note-tomb1-26)[[24]](#cite_note-tomb2-27) The [Easter lily](/wiki/Lilium_longiflorum "Lilium longiflorum"), a symbol of the resurrection in Western Christianity,[[25]](#cite_note-Collins-28)[[26]](#cite_note-Schell_1916_https://archive.org/details/EasterCelebrations/page/n96_84-29) traditionally decorates the [chancel](/wiki/Chancel "Chancel") area of [churches](/wiki/Church_(building) "Church (building)") on this day and for the rest of Eastertide.[[27]](#cite_note-30) In addition to the viewing of [Passion Plays](/wiki/Passion_Play "Passion Play") during Lent and Easter, many television channels air films related to the resurrection, such as *[The Passion of the Christ](/wiki/The_Passion_of_the_Christ "The Passion of the Christ")*, *[The Greatest Story Ever Told](/wiki/The_Greatest_Story_Ever_Told "The Greatest Story Ever Told")* and *[The Jesus Film](/wiki/The_Jesus_Film "The Jesus Film")*.[[28]](#cite_note-31) Additional customs that have become associated with Easter and are observed by both Christians and some non-Christians include [Easter parades](/wiki/Easter_parade "Easter parade"), communal dancing (Eastern Europe), the [Easter Bunny](/wiki/Easter_Bunny "Easter Bunny") and [egg hunting](/wiki/Egg_hunt "Egg hunt").[[29]](#cite_note-Duchak2002-32)[[30]](#cite_note-Sifferlin2015-33)[[31]](#cite_note-Black2004-34)[[32]](#cite_note-35)[[33]](#cite_note-36) There are also traditional [Easter foods](/wiki/Easter_food "Easter food") that vary by region and culture.

## Etymology

Main articles: [Ēostre](/wiki/%C4%92ostre "Ēostre") and [Names of Easter](/wiki/Names_of_Easter "Names of Easter")

The modern English term *Easter*, [cognate](/wiki/Cognate "Cognate") with [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") *Ostern*, developed from an [Old English](/wiki/Old_English_language "Old English language") word that usually appears in the form *Ēastrun*, *Ēastron*, or *Ēastran*; but also as *Ēastru*, *Ēastro*; and *Ēastre* or *Ēostre*.[[d]](#cite_note-37) In the 8th century AD, Anglo-Saxon monk and scholar [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede") recorded in his *[The Reckoning of Time](/wiki/The_Reckoning_of_Time "The Reckoning of Time")* that *Ēosturmōnaþ* ([Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") for 'Month of Ēostre', translated in [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede")'s time as "Paschal month") was an English month, corresponding to April, which he says "was once called after a [goddess](/wiki/List_of_Germanic_deities "List of Germanic deities") of theirs named [Ēostre](/wiki/%C4%92ostre "Ēostre"), in whose honour feasts were celebrated in that month".[[34]](#cite_note-38)

In Latin and Greek, the Christian celebration was, and still is, called *Pascha* (Greek: Πάσχα), a word derived from [Aramaic](/wiki/Aramaic "Aramaic") פסחא (*Paskha*), cognate to the Hebrew פֶּסַח (*Pesach*). The word originally denoted the Jewish festival known in English as [Passover](/wiki/Passover "Passover"), commemorating the [Jewish Exodus from slavery in Egypt](/wiki/The_Exodus "The Exodus").[[35]](#cite_note-HC-39)[[36]](#cite_note-40) As early as 50 AD, [Paul the Apostle](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle"), writing from [Ephesus](/wiki/Ephesus "Ephesus") to the Christians in [Corinth](/wiki/Ancient_Corinth "Ancient Corinth"),[[37]](#cite_note-41) applied the term to Christ. It is unlikely that the [Ephesian and Corinthian Christians](/wiki/Early_Christianity "Early Christianity") were the first to hear Exodus 12 interpreted as speaking about the [death of Jesus](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus"), not just about the Jewish Passover ritual.[[38]](#cite_note-42) In most languages, the feast is known by names derived from the Greek and Latin *Pascha*.[[6]](#cite_note-Etymology-7)[[39]](#cite_note-Passover-43) Pascha is also a name by which Jesus himself is remembered in the Orthodox Church, especially in connection with his resurrection and with the season of its celebration.[[40]](#cite_note-44) Others call the holiday "Resurrection Sunday" or "Resurrection Day", after the Greek [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): Ἀνάστασις (*Anastasis*, 'Resurrection').[[7]](#cite_note-GammanBindon2014-9)[[8]](#cite_note-BodaSmith2006-10)[[41]](#cite_note-45)[[42]](#cite_note-46)

## Theological significance

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/P%C3%B6tting_Kirchenfenster_7_Osterlamm.jpg/250px-P%C3%B6tting_Kirchenfenster_7_Osterlamm.jpg)](/wiki/File:P%C3%B6tting_Kirchenfenster_7_Osterlamm.jpg)

A stained-glass window depicting the [Passover Lamb](/wiki/Lamb_of_God "Lamb of God"), a concept integral to the foundation of Easter[[39]](#cite_note-Passover-43)[[43]](#cite_note-47)

Easter celebrates Jesus' [supernatural resurrection from the dead](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus"), which is one of the chief tenets of the Christian faith.[[44]](#cite_note-48) Paul writes that, for those who trust in Jesus's death and resurrection, "death is swallowed up in victory". The [First Epistle of Peter](/wiki/First_Epistle_of_Peter "First Epistle of Peter") declares that God has given believers "a new birth into a living hope through the resurrection of Jesus Christ from the dead". Christian theology holds that, through faith in the working of God, those who follow Jesus are spiritually resurrected with him so that they may walk in a new way of life and receive eternal [salvation](/wiki/Salvation_in_Christianity "Salvation in Christianity"), and can hope to be physically resurrected to dwell with him in the [Kingdom of Heaven](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Heaven_(Gospel_of_Matthew) "Kingdom of Heaven (Gospel of Matthew)").[[45]](#cite_note-Jesus_EB-49)

Easter is linked to [Passover](/wiki/Passover "Passover") and the [Exodus from Egypt](/wiki/Exodus_from_Egypt "Exodus from Egypt") recorded in the [Old Testament](/wiki/Old_Testament "Old Testament") through the [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper"), [sufferings](/wiki/Passion_of_Jesus "Passion of Jesus"), and [crucifixion of Jesus](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus") that preceded the resurrection.[[39]](#cite_note-Passover-43) According to the three [Synoptic Gospels](/wiki/Synoptic_Gospels "Synoptic Gospels"), Jesus gave the Passover meal a new meaning, as in the [upper room](/wiki/Cenacle "Cenacle") during the Last Supper he prepared himself and his disciples for his death.[[39]](#cite_note-Passover-43) He identified the bread and cup of wine as [his body](/wiki/Body_of_Christ "Body of Christ"), soon to be sacrificed, and [his blood](/wiki/Blood_of_Christ "Blood of Christ"), soon to be shed. The Apostle [Paul](/wiki/Paul_the_Apostle "Paul the Apostle") states in his [First Epistle to the Corinthians](/wiki/First_Epistle_to_the_Corinthians "First Epistle to the Corinthians"): "Get rid of the old yeast that you may be a new batch without yeast—as you really are. For Christ, our Passover lamb, has been sacrificed." This refers to the requirement in Jewish law that Jews eliminate all *[chametz](/wiki/Chametz "Chametz")*, or leavening, from their homes in advance of Passover, and to the allegory of Jesus as the [Passover lamb](/wiki/Korban_Pesach "Korban Pesach").[[46]](#cite_note-50)[[47]](#cite_note-51)

## Early Christianity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/48/The_Last_Supper_-_Leonardo_Da_Vinci_-_High_Resolution_32x16.jpg/250px-The_Last_Supper_-_Leonardo_Da_Vinci_-_High_Resolution_32x16.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Last_Supper_-_Leonardo_Da_Vinci_-_High_Resolution_32x16.jpg)

*[The Last Supper](/wiki/The_Last_Supper_(Leonardo) "The Last Supper (Leonardo)")* (1495–1498). Mural, tempera on gesso, pitch and mastic, 700 x 880 cm (22.9 x 28.8 ft). In the [Santa Maria delle Grazie](/wiki/Santa_Maria_delle_Grazie,_Milan "Santa Maria delle Grazie, Milan") Church, [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), Italy, it is [Leonardo da Vinci](/wiki/Leonardo_da_Vinci "Leonardo da Vinci")'s dramatic interpretation of Jesus' last meal before death. The [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper") celebrated by Jesus and his disciples. The early Christians, too, would have celebrated this meal to commemorate Jesus's death and subsequent resurrection.

As the Gospels assert that both the crucifixion and resurrection of Jesus occurred during the week of Passover, the first Christians timed the observance of the annual celebration of the resurrection in relation to Passover.[[48]](#cite_note-52) Direct evidence for a more fully formed Christian festival of Pascha (Easter) begins to appear in the mid-2nd century. Perhaps the earliest extant primary source referring to Easter is a mid-2nd-century Paschal [homily](/wiki/Homily "Homily") attributed to [Melito of Sardis](/wiki/Melito_of_Sardis "Melito of Sardis"), which characterizes the celebration as a well-established one.[[49]](#cite_note-Melito-53) Evidence for another kind of annually recurring Christian festival, those commemorating the martyrs, began to appear at about the same time as the above homily.[[50]](#cite_note-54)

While martyrs' days (usually the individual dates of martyrdom) were celebrated on fixed dates in the local solar calendar, the date of Easter was fixed by means of the local Jewish[[51]](#cite_note-55) [lunisolar calendar](/wiki/Lunisolar_calendar "Lunisolar calendar"). This is consistent with the celebration of Easter having entered Christianity during its earliest, [Jewish period](/wiki/Jewish_Christianity "Jewish Christianity").[[52]](#cite_note-56)

## Date

Main articles: [Date of Easter](/wiki/Date_of_Easter "Date of Easter") and [List of dates for Easter](/wiki/List_of_dates_for_Easter "List of dates for Easter")

Easter and the holidays that are related to it are [moveable feasts](/wiki/Moveable_feast "Moveable feast"), in that they do not fall on a fixed date in the [Gregorian](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar") or [Julian](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar") calendars (both of which follow the cycle of the sun and the seasons). Instead, the date for Easter is determined on a [lunisolar calendar](/wiki/Lunisolar_calendar "Lunisolar calendar") similar to the [Hebrew calendar](/wiki/Hebrew_calendar "Hebrew calendar").

### Early Church controversies

Main article: [Easter controversy](/wiki/Easter_controversy "Easter controversy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/5part-icon.jpeg/250px-5part-icon.jpeg)](/wiki/File:5part-icon.jpeg)

A five-part [Russian Orthodox](/wiki/Russian_Orthodox "Russian Orthodox") icon depicting the Easter story. [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") Christians use a different computation for the date of Easter from the Western churches.

The precise date of Easter has at times been a matter of contention. By the later 2nd century, it was widely accepted that the celebration of the holiday was a practice of the [disciples](/wiki/Disciple_(Christianity) "Disciple (Christianity)") and an undisputed tradition. The [Quartodeciman](/wiki/Quartodecimanism "Quartodecimanism") controversy, the first of several [Easter controversies](/wiki/Easter_controversy "Easter controversy"), arose concerning the date on which the holiday should be celebrated.[[53]](#cite_note-NEW_ADVENT_1909-57)

The term "Quartodeciman" refers to the practice of ending the Lenten fast on [Nisan](/wiki/Nisan "Nisan") 14 of the [Hebrew calendar](/wiki/Hebrew_calendar "Hebrew calendar"), "the LORD's passover".[[54]](#cite_note-58) According to the church historian [Eusebius](/wiki/Eusebius_of_Caesarea "Eusebius of Caesarea"), the Quartodeciman [Polycarp](/wiki/Polycarp "Polycarp") (bishop of [Smyrna](/wiki/Smyrna "Smyrna"), by tradition a disciple of [John the Apostle](/wiki/John_the_Apostle "John the Apostle")) debated the question with [Anicetus](/wiki/Pope_Anicetus "Pope Anicetus") (bishop of Rome). The [Roman province of Asia](/wiki/Asia_(Roman_province) "Asia (Roman province)") was Quartodeciman, while the Roman and Alexandrian churches continued the fast until the Sunday following (the Sunday of Unleavened Bread), wishing to associate Easter with Sunday. Neither Polycarp nor Anicetus persuaded the other, but they did not consider the matter [schismatic](/wiki/Schism_(religion) "Schism (religion)") either, parting in peace and leaving the question unsettled.[[55]](#cite_note-Christian_Classics_Ethereal_Library_2-59)

Controversy arose when [Victor](/wiki/Pope_Victor_I "Pope Victor I"), bishop of Rome a generation after Anicetus, attempted to [excommunicate](/wiki/Excommunication "Excommunication") [Polycrates of Ephesus](/wiki/Polycrates_of_Ephesus "Polycrates of Ephesus") and all other bishops of Asia for their Quartodecimanism. According to Eusebius, a number of synods were convened to deal with the controversy, which he regarded as all ruling in support of Easter on Sunday.[[56]](#cite_note-60) Polycrates (c. 190), however, wrote to Victor defending the antiquity of Asian Quartodecimanism. Victor's attempted excommunication was apparently rescinded, and the two sides reconciled upon the intervention of bishop [Irenaeus](/wiki/Irenaeus "Irenaeus") and others, who reminded Victor of the tolerant precedent of Anicetus.[[57]](#cite_note-Kelly_1978_p.-61)[[58]](#cite_note-Grace_Communion_International_2018-62)

Quartodecimanism seems to have lingered into the 4th century, when [Socrates of Constantinople](/wiki/Socrates_of_Constantinople "Socrates of Constantinople") recorded that some Quartodecimans were deprived of their churches by [John Chrysostom](/wiki/John_Chrysostom "John Chrysostom")[[59]](#cite_note-63) and that some were harassed by [Nestorius](/wiki/Nestorius "Nestorius").[[60]](#cite_note-64)

It is not known how long the Nisan 14 practice continued. But both those who followed the Nisan 14 custom, and those who set Easter to the following Sunday, had in common the custom of consulting their Jewish neighbors to learn when the month of Nisan would fall, and setting their festival accordingly. By the later 3rd century, however, some Christians began to express dissatisfaction with the custom of relying on the Jewish community to determine the date of Easter. The chief complaint was that the Jewish communities sometimes erred in setting Passover to fall before the [Northern Hemisphere](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere") spring equinox.[[61]](#cite_note-65)[[62]](#cite_note-66) The [Sardica paschal table](/wiki/Sardica_paschal_table "Sardica paschal table")[[63]](#cite_note-67) confirms these complaints, for it indicates that the Jews of some eastern Mediterranean city (possibly [Antioch](/wiki/Antioch "Antioch")) fixed Nisan 14 on dates well before the spring equinox on multiple occasions.[[64]](#cite_note-68)

Because of this dissatisfaction with reliance on the Jewish calendar, some Christians began to experiment with independent computations.[[e]](#cite_note-71) Others, however, believed that the customary practice of consulting Jews should continue, even if the Jewish computations were in error.[[67]](#cite_note-NEW_ADVENT_(Church_Fathers)-72)

### First Council of Nicaea (325 AD)

Main article: [First Council of Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Nikea-arius.png/250px-Nikea-arius.png)](/wiki/File:Nikea-arius.png)

The [First Council of Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea"), with Arius depicted as defeated by the council, lying under the feet of [Emperor Constantine](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great")

The settlement of the [controversy about the Paschal season](/wiki/Easter_controversy "Easter controversy") caused by the [Quartodeciman](/wiki/Quartodeciman "Quartodeciman") practice of Asian [churches](/wiki/Church_(congregation) "Church (congregation)") is listed in our principal source for the works of the [Council of Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea"), [Socrates Scholasticus](/wiki/Socrates_Scholasticus "Socrates Scholasticus")'s *Ecclesiastical History*, as one of the two reasons for which emperor [Constantine](/wiki/Constantine_the_Great "Constantine the Great") convened the Council in 325.[[68]](#cite_note-73) The Canons of the Council preserved by [Dionysius Exiguus](/wiki/Dionysius_Exiguus "Dionysius Exiguus") and his successors do not include any relevant provision, but letters of individuals present at the Council mention a decision prohibiting Quartodecimanism and requiring that all Christians adopt a common method to independently determine Paschal observance following the churches of Rome and Alexandria, the latter "since [there was among the Egyptians an ancient science for the computation](/wiki/Egyptian_astronomy "Egyptian astronomy")."[[69]](#cite_note-74)
Already in the end of the 4th century and, later on, [Dionysius Exiguus](/wiki/Dionysius_Exiguus "Dionysius Exiguus") and others following him maintained that the bishops assembled at Nicaea had promulgated the celebration of Easter on the first Sunday after the first full moon on or after the vernal equinox and that they had adopted the use of the 19-year lunar cycle, better known as [Metonic cycle](/wiki/Metonic_cycle "Metonic cycle"), to determine the date; subsequent scholarship has refuted this tradition, but, with regards to the rule of the equinox, evidence that the church of Alexandria had implemented it before 325 suggests that the Council of Nicaea implicitly endorsed it.[[70]](#cite_note-75)

Canons[[71]](#cite_note-76) and sermons[[72]](#cite_note-77) condemning the custom of computing Easter's date based on the Jewish calendar indicate that this custom (called "protopaschite" by historians) did not die out at once, but persisted for a time after the Council of Nicaea.[[73]](#cite_note-McGuckin_2011_p.223-78) In any case, in the years following the council, the computational system that was worked out by the church of Alexandria came to be normative. The Alexandrian system, however, was not immediately adopted throughout Christian Europe. Following [Augustalis](/wiki/Augustalis_(bishop) "Augustalis (bishop)")' treatise *De ratione Paschae* (On the Measurement of Easter), Rome retired the earlier [8-year cycle](/wiki/Octaeteris "Octaeteris") in favor of Augustalis' 84-year [lunisolar calendar](/wiki/Lunisolar_calendar "Lunisolar calendar") cycle, which it used until 457. It then switched to [Victorius of Aquitaine](/wiki/Victorius_of_Aquitaine "Victorius of Aquitaine")'s adaptation of the Alexandrian system.[[74]](#cite_note-Mosshammer_2008_239–244-79)[[75]](#cite_note-Holford-Strevens,_Leofranc_1999_808–809-80)

Because this Victorian cycle differed from the unmodified Alexandrian cycle in the dates of some of the Paschal full moons, and because it tried to respect the Roman custom of fixing Easter to the Sunday in the week of the 16th to the 22nd of the lunar month (rather than the 15th to the 21st as at Alexandria), by providing alternative "Latin" and "Greek" dates in some years, occasional differences in the date of Easter as fixed by Alexandrian rules continued.[[74]](#cite_note-Mosshammer_2008_239–244-79)[[75]](#cite_note-Holford-Strevens,_Leofranc_1999_808–809-80) The Alexandrian rules were adopted in the West following the tables of Dionysius Exiguus in 525.[[76]](#cite_note-Declercq_2000_p.143-144-81)

Early Christians in Britain and Ireland also used an 84-year cycle. From the 5th century onward this cycle set its equinox to 25 March and fixed Easter to the Sunday falling in the 14th to the 20th of the lunar month inclusive.[[77]](#cite_note-82)[[78]](#cite_note-83) This 84-year cycle was replaced by the Alexandrian method in the course of the 7th and 8th centuries. Churches in western continental Europe used a late Roman method until the late 8th century during the reign of [Charlemagne](/wiki/Charlemagne "Charlemagne"), when they finally adopted the Alexandrian method. Since 1582, when the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") adopted the Gregorian calendar while most of Europe used the Julian calendar, the date on which Easter is celebrated has again differed.[[79]](#cite_note-84)

### Computations

See also: [Computus](/wiki/Computus "Computus")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Calendario_pasquale.jpg/250px-Calendario_pasquale.jpg)](/wiki/File:Calendario_pasquale.jpg)

A calendar of the dates of Easter, for the 95 years 532–626, marble, in the Museum of [Ravenna Cathedral](/wiki/Ravenna_Cathedral "Ravenna Cathedral"), Italy. Five 19-year cycles are represented as concentric circles. Dates are given using the system of the [Roman calendar](/wiki/Roman_calendar "Roman calendar"), as well as the day of the lunar month.

In 725, [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede") succinctly wrote: "The Sunday following the full Moon which falls on or after the [equinox](/wiki/Equinox "Equinox") will give the lawful Easter."[[80]](#cite_note-85) However, this does not precisely reflect the ecclesiastical rules. The full moon referred to (called the [Paschal full moon](/wiki/Paschal_full_moon "Paschal full moon")) is not an astronomical full moon, but the [14th day](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_full_moon "Ecclesiastical full moon") of a [lunar month](/wiki/Lunar_month "Lunar month"). Another difference is that the [astronomical equinox](/wiki/Equinox "Equinox") is a natural astronomical phenomenon, which can fall on 19, 20, or 21 March,[[81]](#cite_note-86) while the ecclesiastical date is fixed by convention on 21 March.[[82]](#cite_note-87)

In addition, the lunar tables of the Julian calendar are currently five days behind those of the Gregorian calendar. Therefore, the Julian computation of the Paschal full moon is a full five days later than the astronomical full moon. The result of this combination of solar and lunar discrepancies is divergence in the date of Easter in most years (see table).[[83]](#cite_note-dateACC-88)

Easter is determined on the basis of [lunisolar](/wiki/Lunisolar_calendar "Lunisolar calendar") cycles. The lunar year consists of 30-day and 29-day lunar months, generally alternating, with an [embolismic month](/wiki/Embolismic_month "Embolismic month") added periodically to bring the lunar cycle into line with the solar cycle. In each solar year (1 January to 31 December inclusive), the lunar month beginning with an [ecclesiastical new moon](/wiki/Ecclesiastical_new_moon "Ecclesiastical new moon") falling in the 29-day period from 8 March to 5 April inclusive is designated as the paschal lunar month for that year.[[84]](#cite_note-smart.net-89)

Easter is the third Sunday in the paschal lunar month, or, in other words, the Sunday after the paschal lunar month's 14th day. The 14th of the paschal lunar month is designated by convention as the [Paschal full moon](/wiki/Paschal_full_moon "Paschal full moon"), although the 14th of the lunar month may differ from the date of the astronomical full moon by up to two days.[[84]](#cite_note-smart.net-89) Since the ecclesiastical new moon falls on a date from 8 March to 5 April inclusive, the paschal full moon (the 14th of that lunar month) must fall on a date from 22 March to 18 April inclusive.[[83]](#cite_note-dateACC-88)

The Gregorian calculation of Easter was based on a method devised by the [Calabrian](/wiki/Calabria "Calabria") doctor [Aloysius Lilius](/wiki/Aloysius_Lilius "Aloysius Lilius") (or Lilio) for adjusting the [epacts](/wiki/Epact "Epact") of the Moon,[[85]](#cite_note-90) and has been adopted by almost all Western Christians and by Western countries which celebrate national holidays at Easter. For the British Empire and colonies, a determination of the date of Easter Sunday using [Golden Numbers](/wiki/Golden_number_(time) "Golden number (time)") and [Sunday letters](/wiki/Dominical_letter "Dominical letter") was defined by the [Calendar (New Style) Act 1750](/wiki/Calendar_(New_Style)_Act_1750 "Calendar (New Style) Act 1750") with its Annexe. This was designed to match exactly the Gregorian calculation.[[86]](#cite_note-legislation.gov.uk-calendar-91)

### Western-Eastern divergence

In Western Christianity, using the Gregorian calendar, Easter always falls on a Sunday between 22 March and 25 April,[[87]](#cite_note-92) within about seven days after the astronomical full moon.[[88]](#cite_note-93) The preceding Friday, [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday"), and following Monday, [Easter Monday](/wiki/Easter_Monday "Easter Monday"), are [legal holidays](/wiki/Legal_holiday "Legal holiday") in many countries with predominantly Christian traditions.[[89]](#cite_note-94)

[Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy "Eastern Orthodoxy") Christians use the same rule but base their 21 March according to the Julian calendar. Because of the thirteen-day difference between the calendars from 1900 through 2099, 21 March Julian corresponds to 3 April in the Gregorian calendar (during the 20th and 21st centuries). Consequently, the date of Orthodox Easter varies between 4 April and 8 May in the Gregorian calendar.

Furthermore, because the lunar tables of the Julian calendar are five days behind astronomical reality, the full moon, as defined by the Eastern Orthodox Church, is five days later, on a day which actually is a waning gibbous moon. Therefore, if a Sunday falls on any of the four days after the astronomical full moon, this is regarded as occurring before the ecclesiastical full moon and Easter Sunday will be a week later.

As a result, Orthodox Easter may fall on the same day as Catholic Easter, a week later, four weeks later, or five weeks later (but at no other time intervals, at least until the 25th century). Easter is celebrated on the same day in years in which the vernal full moon occurs late enough to be after the defined equinoxes of both churches and on a Sunday, Monday, or Tuesday. This is the case for 31 years of the 21st century, but the frequency is decreasing. (It occurred in 47 years in the 17th century, but will happen for the last time in 2698, after which the Easters will be permanently on different dates). It falls one week after Catholic Easter when vernal full moon occurs Wednesday through Saturday, or four or five weeks after Catholic Easter when the Catholic vernal full moon falls before or around 29 March, depending on the day of the week.[[90]](#cite_note-95)[[91]](#cite_note-96)

Among the [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodoxy "Oriental Orthodoxy"), some churches have changed from the Julian to the Gregorian calendar and the date for Easter, as for other fixed and moveable feasts, is the same as in the Western church.[[92]](#cite_note-97)

The Greek island of [Syros](/wiki/Syros "Syros"), whose population is divided almost equally between Catholics and Orthodox, is one of the few places where the two Churches share a common date for Easter, with the Catholics accepting the Orthodox date—a practice helping considerably in maintaining good relations between the two communities.[[93]](#cite_note-98) Conversely, Orthodox Christians in Finland celebrate Easter according to the [Western Christian](/wiki/Western_Christian "Western Christian") date.[[94]](#cite_note-99)

In the 20th and 21st centuries, some individuals and institutions have propounded changing the method of calculating the date for Easter, the most prominent proposal being the Sunday after the second Saturday in April. Despite having some support, proposals to reform the date have not been implemented.[[95]](#cite_note-Britannica-100) An Orthodox congress of Eastern Orthodox bishops, which included representatives mostly from the [Patriarch of Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarch_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople") and the [Serbian Patriarch](/wiki/Serbian_Patriarch "Serbian Patriarch"), met in [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople") in 1923, where the bishops agreed to the [Revised Julian calendar](/wiki/Revised_Julian_calendar "Revised Julian calendar").[[96]](#cite_note-Hieromonk_Cassian_1998,_p.51-101)

The original form of this calendar would have determined Easter using precise astronomical calculations based on the meridian of [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem_in_Christianity "Jerusalem in Christianity").[[97]](#cite_note-102)[[98]](#cite_note-103) However, all the Eastern Orthodox countries that subsequently adopted the Revised Julian calendar adopted only that part of the revised calendar that applied to festivals falling on fixed dates in the Julian calendar. The revised Easter computation that had been part of the original 1923 agreement was never permanently implemented in any Orthodox diocese.[[96]](#cite_note-Hieromonk_Cassian_1998,_p.51-101)

In the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") passed the [Easter Act 1928](/wiki/Easter_Act_1928 "Easter Act 1928") to change the date of Easter to be the first Sunday after the second Saturday in April (or, in other words, the Sunday in the period from 9 to 15 April). However, the legislation has not been implemented, although it remains on the Statute book and could be implemented, subject to approval by the various Christian churches.[[99]](#cite_note-104)

At a summit in [Aleppo](/wiki/Aleppo "Aleppo"), Syria, in 1997, the [World Council of Churches](/wiki/World_Council_of_Churches "World Council of Churches") (WCC) proposed a [reform in the calculation of Easter](/wiki/Aleppo_Easter_dating_method "Aleppo Easter dating method") which would have replaced the present divergent practices of calculating Easter with modern scientific knowledge taking into account actual astronomical instances of the spring equinox and full moon based on the meridian of Jerusalem, while also following the tradition of Easter being on the Sunday following the full moon.[[100]](#cite_note-105) The recommended World Council of Churches changes would have sidestepped the calendar issues and eliminated the difference in date between the Eastern and Western churches. The reform was proposed for implementation starting in 2001, and despite repeated calls for reform, it was not ultimately adopted by any member body.[[101]](#cite_note-106)[[102]](#cite_note-107)

In January 2016, the [Anglican Communion](/wiki/Anglican_Communion "Anglican Communion"), [Coptic Orthodox Church](/wiki/Coptic_Orthodox_Church "Coptic Orthodox Church"), [Greek Orthodox Church](/wiki/Greek_Orthodox_Church "Greek Orthodox Church"), and Roman Catholic Church again considered agreeing on a common, universal date for Easter, while also simplifying the calculation of that date, with either the second or third Sunday in April being popular choices.[[103]](#cite_note-108)

In November 2022, the Patriarch of Constantinople said that conversations between the Roman Catholic Church and the Orthodox Churches had begun to determine a common date for the celebration of Easter. The agreement is expected to be reached for the 1700th anniversary of the Council of Nicaea in 2025.[[104]](#cite_note-109)
