## Position in the church year

Further information: [Liturgical year](/wiki/Liturgical_year "Liturgical year")

### Western Christianity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0a/Lent_calendar.svg/500px-Lent_calendar.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Lent_calendar.svg)

*Easter* and other named days and day ranges around Lent and Easter in Western Christianity, with the fasting days of Lent numbered

In most branches of Western Christianity, Easter is preceded by [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"), a period of penitence that begins on [Ash Wednesday](/wiki/Ash_Wednesday "Ash Wednesday"), lasts 40 days (not counting Sundays), and is often marked with fasting. The week before Easter, known as [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week"), is an important time for observers to commemorate the final week of Jesus' life on earth.[[106]](#cite_note-113) The Sunday before Easter is [Palm Sunday](/wiki/Palm_Sunday "Palm Sunday"), with the Wednesday before Easter being known as [Spy Wednesday](/wiki/Spy_Wednesday "Spy Wednesday") (or Holy Wednesday). The last [three days](/wiki/The_Three_Days "The Three Days") before Easter are [Maundy Thursday](/wiki/Maundy_Thursday "Maundy Thursday"), [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday") and [Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday") (sometimes referred to as Silent Saturday).[[107]](#cite_note-114)

Palm Sunday, Maundy Thursday and Good Friday respectively commemorate Jesus's entry in Jerusalem, the [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper") and the [crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion "Crucifixion"). Maundy Thursday, Good Friday, and Holy Saturday are sometimes referred to as the [Easter Triduum](/wiki/Easter_Triduum "Easter Triduum") ([Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") for "Three Days"). Many churches begin celebrating Easter late in the evening of Holy Saturday at a service called the [Easter Vigil](/wiki/Easter_Vigil "Easter Vigil").[[108]](#cite_note-Encyclopedia_Britannica_1998_(Holy_Saturday)-115)

The week beginning with Easter Sunday is called Easter Week or the [Octave of Easter](/wiki/Octave_of_Easter "Octave of Easter"), and each day is prefaced with "Easter", e.g. [Easter Monday](/wiki/Easter_Monday "Easter Monday") (a public holiday in many countries), [Easter Tuesday](/wiki/Easter_Tuesday "Easter Tuesday") (a much less widespread public holiday), etc. [Easter Saturday](/wiki/Easter_Saturday "Easter Saturday") is therefore the Saturday *after* Easter Sunday. The day before Easter is properly called Holy Saturday. [Eastertide](/wiki/Eastertide "Eastertide"), or Paschaltide, the season of Easter, begins on Easter Sunday and lasts until the day of [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost"), seven weeks later.[[109]](#cite_note-Fairchild_2012-116)[[110]](#cite_note-Bucher_2021-117)[[111]](#cite_note-Huck_Ramshaw_Lathrop_1988_p.-118)

### Eastern Christianity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Christ_Pantocrator_mosaic_from_Hagia_Sophia_2744_x_2900_pixels_3.1_MB.jpg/250px-Christ_Pantocrator_mosaic_from_Hagia_Sophia_2744_x_2900_pixels_3.1_MB.jpg)](/wiki/File:Christ_Pantocrator_mosaic_from_Hagia_Sophia_2744_x_2900_pixels_3.1_MB.jpg)

Mosaic of [Christ Pantocrator](/wiki/Christ_Pantocrator "Christ Pantocrator"), [Hagia Sophia](/wiki/Hagia_Sophia "Hagia Sophia")

In [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity"), the spiritual preparation for Easter/Pascha begins with [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent"), which starts on [Clean Monday](/wiki/Clean_Monday "Clean Monday") and lasts for 40 continuous days (including Sundays). Great Lent ends on a Friday, and the next day is [Lazarus Saturday](/wiki/Lazarus_Saturday "Lazarus Saturday"). The [Vespers](/wiki/Vespers "Vespers") which begins Lazarus Saturday officially brings Great Lent to a close, although the fast continues through the following week.[[112]](#cite_note-BBC_2002-119)[[113]](#cite_note-McGuckin_2011_p.-120)

The [Paschal Vigil](/wiki/Paschal_Vigil "Paschal Vigil") begins with the [Midnight Office](/wiki/Midnight_Office "Midnight Office"), which is the last service of the [Lenten Triodion](/wiki/Lenten_Triodion "Lenten Triodion") and is timed so that it ends a little before midnight on [Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday") night. At the stroke of midnight the Paschal celebration itself begins, consisting of Paschal [Matins](/wiki/Matins "Matins"), [Paschal Hours](/wiki/Paschal_Hours "Paschal Hours"), and Paschal [Divine Liturgy](/wiki/Divine_Liturgy "Divine Liturgy").[[114]](#cite_note-Eastern_Liturgy-121)

The liturgical season from Easter to the Sunday of [All Saints](/wiki/All_Saints%27_Day "All Saints' Day") (the Sunday after [Pentecost](/wiki/Pentecost "Pentecost")) is known as the [Pentecostarion](/wiki/Pentecostarion "Pentecostarion") (the "50 days"). The week which begins on Easter Sunday is called [Bright Week](/wiki/Bright_Week "Bright Week"), during which there is no fasting, even on Wednesday and Friday. The [Afterfeast](/wiki/Afterfeast "Afterfeast") of Easter lasts 39 days, with its [Apodosis](/wiki/Afterfeast "Afterfeast") (leave-taking) on the day before the [Feast of the Ascension](/wiki/Feast_of_the_Ascension "Feast of the Ascension"). Pentecost Sunday is the 50th day from Easter (counted inclusively).[[115]](#cite_note-122) In the Pentecostarion published by Apostoliki Diakonia of the Church of Greece, the Great Feast Pentecost is noted in the synaxarion portion of Matins to be the 8th Sunday of Pascha. However, the [Paschal greeting](/wiki/Paschal_greeting "Paschal greeting") of "Christ is risen!" is no longer exchanged among the faithful after the Apodosis of Pascha.[[116]](#cite_note-Holy_Transfiguration_Monastery-123)[[117]](#cite_note-melkite.org_2023-124)

## Liturgical observance

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6f/Easter_Sunday_2016.jpg/250px-Easter_Sunday_2016.jpg)](/wiki/File:Easter_Sunday_2016.jpg)

Christian worshippers attend an Easter Sunday [church service](/wiki/Church_service "Church service") at [St James's Church, Piccadilly](/wiki/St_James%27s_Church,_Piccadilly "St James's Church, Piccadilly"), London. The cross in the [chancel](/wiki/Chancel "Chancel") is draped with a white [shroud](/wiki/Shroud "Shroud"), symbolizing the resurrection.[[118]](#cite_note-SL2023-125)[[119]](#cite_note-126)

### Western Christianity

The Easter festival is kept in many different ways among Western Christians. The traditional, [liturgical](/wiki/Liturgy "Liturgy") observation of Easter, as practised among Roman Catholics, [Lutherans](/wiki/Lutheran "Lutheran"),[[120]](#cite_note-127) and some [Anglicans](/wiki/Anglican "Anglican") begins on the night of [Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday") with the [Easter Vigil](/wiki/Easter_Vigil "Easter Vigil") which follows an ancient liturgy involving symbols of light, candles and water and numerous readings from the Old and New Testament.[[121]](#cite_note-128)

Services continue on Easter Sunday and in a number of countries on [Easter Monday](/wiki/Easter_Monday "Easter Monday"). In parishes of the [Moravian Church](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church"), as well as some other denominations such as the [Methodist Churches](/wiki/Methodist_Church "Methodist Church"), there is a tradition of Easter [sunrise services](/wiki/Sunrise_service "Sunrise service"),[[122]](#cite_note-129) often starting in [cemeteries](/wiki/God%27s_Acre "God's Acre")[[123]](#cite_note-130) in remembrance of the biblical narrative in the Gospels, or other places in the open where the sunrise is visible.[[124]](#cite_note-131)

In some traditions, Easter services typically begin with the [Paschal greeting](/wiki/Paschal_greeting "Paschal greeting"): "Christ is risen!" The response is: "He is risen indeed. Alleluia!"[[125]](#cite_note-132)

### Eastern Christianity

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Receiving_the_Holy_Light_at_Easter.jpg/250px-Receiving_the_Holy_Light_at_Easter.jpg)](/wiki/File:Receiving_the_Holy_Light_at_Easter.jpg)

The congregation lighting their candles from the new flame, just as the priest has retrieved it from the altar. The picture is [flash-illuminated](/wiki/Flash_(photography) "Flash (photography)"); all electric lighting is off, and only the [oil lamps](/wiki/Oil_lamp "Oil lamp") in front of the [Iconostasis](/wiki/Iconostasis "Iconostasis") remain lit. ([St. George Greek Orthodox Church](/wiki/Community_and_Parish_of_Saint_George_Thebarton,_Adelaide,_South_Australia "Community and Parish of Saint George Thebarton, Adelaide, South Australia"), Adelaide).

[Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), [Eastern Catholics](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches") and [Byzantine Rite Lutherans](/wiki/Byzantine_Rite_Lutheranism "Byzantine Rite Lutheranism") have a similar emphasis on Easter in their calendars, and many of their liturgical customs are very similar.[[126]](#cite_note-Moroz-133)

Preparation for Easter begins with the season of [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent"), which begins on [Clean Monday](/wiki/Clean_Monday "Clean Monday").[[127]](#cite_note-histchan-134) While the end of Lent is [Lazarus Saturday](/wiki/Lazarus_Saturday "Lazarus Saturday"), fasting does not end until Easter Sunday.[[128]](#cite_note-easter_looks_different-135) The Orthodox service begins late Saturday evening, observing the Jewish tradition that evening is the start of liturgical holy days.[[128]](#cite_note-easter_looks_different-135)

The church is darkened, then the priest lights a candle at midnight, representing the resurrection of Jesus Christ. Altar servers light additional candles, with a procession which moves three times around the church to represent the three days in the tomb.[[128]](#cite_note-easter_looks_different-135) The service continues early into Sunday morning, with a feast to end the fasting. An additional service is held later that day on Easter Sunday.[[128]](#cite_note-easter_looks_different-135)

### Non-observing Christian groups

Many [Puritans](/wiki/Puritan "Puritan") saw traditional feasts of the established Anglican Church, such as All Saints' Day and Easter, as [abominations](/wiki/Abomination_(Bible) "Abomination (Bible)") because the Bible does not mention them.[[129]](#cite_note-Daniels89-136)[[130]](#cite_note-137) Conservative Reformed denominations such as the [Free Presbyterian Church of Scotland](/wiki/Free_Presbyterian_Church_of_Scotland "Free Presbyterian Church of Scotland") and the [Reformed Presbyterian Church of North America](/wiki/Reformed_Presbyterian_Church_of_North_America "Reformed Presbyterian Church of North America") likewise reject the celebration of Easter as a violation of the [regulative principle of worship](/wiki/Regulative_principle_of_worship "Regulative principle of worship") and what they see as its non-Scriptural origin.[[131]](#cite_note-138)[[132]](#cite_note-139)

Easter is rejected by groups such as the [Restored Church of God](/wiki/Restored_Church_of_God "Restored Church of God"), who claim it originated as a pagan spring festival adopted by the Roman Catholic Church.[[133]](#cite_note-140)[[134]](#cite_note-Okogba_2019-141)

[Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses") maintain a similar view, observing a yearly commemorative service of the [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper") and the subsequent execution of Christ on the evening of Nisan 14 (as they calculate the dates derived from the lunar [Hebrew calendar](/wiki/Hebrew_calendar "Hebrew calendar")). It is commonly referred to by many Witnesses as simply "[The Memorial](/wiki/Memorial_(Jehovah%27s_Witnesses) "Memorial (Jehovah's Witnesses)")". Jehovah's Witnesses believe that such verses as [Luke 22:19–20](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/Luke#22:19 "s:Bible (King James)/Luke") and [1 Corinthians 11:26](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/Bible_(King_James)/1_Corinthians#11:26 "s:Bible (King James)/1 Corinthians") constitute a commandment to remember the death of Christ, though not the resurrection.[[135]](#cite_note-BBC_2006-142)[[136]](#cite_note-jw.org-143)

Members of the [Religious Society of Friends (Quakers)](/wiki/Religious_Society_of_Friends "Religious Society of Friends"), as part of their historic *testimony against times and seasons*, do not celebrate or observe Easter or any traditional feast days of the established Church, believing instead that "every day is the [Lord's Day](/wiki/Lord%27s_Day "Lord's Day")", and that elevation of one day above others suggests that it is acceptable to do un-Christian acts on other days.[[137]](#cite_note-144)[[138]](#cite_note-145) During the 17th and 18th centuries, Quakers were persecuted for this non-observance of Holy Days.[[139]](#cite_note-146)

## Easter celebrations around the world

Main article: [Easter traditions](/wiki/Easter_traditions "Easter traditions")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Velika_no%C4%8D_-_jedila_hren_%C5%A1unka_pirhi_potica.jpg/250px-Velika_no%C4%8D_-_jedila_hren_%C5%A1unka_pirhi_potica.jpg)](/wiki/File:Velika_no%C4%8D_-_jedila_hren_%C5%A1unka_pirhi_potica.jpg)

Traditional [Slovenian](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") Easter breakfast with eggs, ham with horseradish, and [potica](/wiki/Potica "Potica")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Easter_Colors.svg/250px-Easter_Colors.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Easter_Colors.svg)

Pastel colors are commonly associated with Easter.[[140]](#cite_note-147)

[Easter traditions](/wiki/Easter_traditions "Easter traditions") (also known as Paschal traditions) are customs and practices that are followed in various cultures and communities around the world to celebrate Easter, which is the central feast in [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), commemorating the [resurrection of Jesus](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus"). The [Easter season](/wiki/Eastertide "Eastertide") is seen as a time of celebration and feasting, in contrast to the antecedent season of [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent"), which is a time of penitence and fasting.[[141]](#cite_note-PennoyerWolfe2015-148)

Easter traditions include [sunrise services](/wiki/Sunrise_service "Sunrise service") or [late-night vigils](/wiki/Easter_Vigil "Easter Vigil"), exclamations and exchanges of [Paschal greetings](/wiki/Paschal_greeting "Paschal greeting"), [flowering the cross](/wiki/Flowering_the_cross "Flowering the cross"),[[20]](#cite_note-Whitehouse2022-23) the wearing of [Easter bonnets](/wiki/Easter_bonnet "Easter bonnet") by women,[[142]](#cite_note-Friedman2016-149) [clipping the church](/wiki/Clipping_the_church "Clipping the church"),[[21]](#cite_note-clipping_the_church-24) and the [decoration](/wiki/Egg_decorating "Egg decorating") and the communal breaking of [Easter eggs](/wiki/Easter_egg "Easter egg") (a symbol of the [empty tomb](/wiki/Empty_tomb "Empty tomb")).[[22]](#cite_note-Jordan2000-25)[[23]](#cite_note-tomb1-26)[[24]](#cite_note-tomb2-27) The [Easter lily](/wiki/Lilium_longiflorum "Lilium longiflorum"), a symbol of the resurrection in Christianity,[[25]](#cite_note-Collins-28)[[26]](#cite_note-Schell_1916_https://archive.org/details/EasterCelebrations/page/n96_84-29) traditionally decorates the [chancel](/wiki/Chancel "Chancel") area of [churches](/wiki/Church_(building) "Church (building)") on this day and for the rest of [Eastertide](/wiki/Eastertide "Eastertide").[[143]](#cite_note-150) There are also traditional [Easter foods](/wiki/Easter_food "Easter food") that vary by region and culture. Many traditional Easter games and customs developed, such as [egg rolling](/wiki/Egg_rolling "Egg rolling"), [egg tapping](/wiki/Egg_tapping "Egg tapping"), and [cascarones or confetti eggs](/wiki/Cascar%C3%B3n "Cascarón").[[144]](#cite_note-Hunt2016-151) [Egg hunting](/wiki/Egg_hunt "Egg hunt"), originating in the idea of searching for the empty tomb, is an activity that remains popular among children.[[144]](#cite_note-Hunt2016-151)[[31]](#cite_note-Black2004-34)[[145]](#cite_note-Davis-152) Today Easter is commercially important, seeing wide sales of [greeting cards](/wiki/Greeting_card "Greeting card") and confectionery such as chocolate [Easter eggs](/wiki/Easter_eggs_(decorative) "Easter eggs (decorative)").

In countries where Christianity is a [state religion](/wiki/State_religion "State religion"), or those with large Christian populations, Easter is often a [public holiday](/wiki/Public_holiday "Public holiday").[[146]](#cite_note-Agency_2016-153) As Easter always falls on a Sunday, many countries in the world also recognize [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday") and Easter Monday as public holidays.[[147]](#cite_note-Acevedo_2023-154) Depending on the country, retail stores, shopping malls and restaurants may be closed on the Friday, Monday or Sunday.[[148]](#cite_note-Uro_Day_DeMaris_Roitto_2019_p.-155)

In the [Nordic countries](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries"), Good Friday, Easter Sunday, and Easter Monday are public holidays,[[149]](#cite_note-156) and Good Friday and Easter Monday are bank holidays.[[150]](#cite_note-157) In Denmark, Iceland and Norway, Maundy Thursday is also a public holiday; it is a holiday for most workers, except those operating some shopping malls which keep open for a half-day. Many businesses give their employees almost a week off, called Easter break.[[151]](#cite_note-158) Schools are closed between Palm Sunday and Easter Monday. According to a 2014 poll, 6 of 10 Norwegians travel during Easter, often to a countryside cottage; 3 of 10 said their typical Easter included skiing.[[152]](#cite_note-159)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6d/Otto_Santi_Ruvo_2023.jpg/250px-Otto_Santi_Ruvo_2023.jpg)](/wiki/File:Otto_Santi_Ruvo_2023.jpg)

[Holy Week in Ruvo di Puglia](/wiki/Holy_Week_in_Ruvo_di_Puglia "Holy Week in Ruvo di Puglia"), [Apulia](/wiki/Apulia "Apulia"), Italy

[Easter in Italy](/wiki/Easter_in_Italy "Easter in Italy") is one of that country's major holidays.[[153]](#cite_note-160) Easter in Italy enters [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week") with Palm Sunday, Maundy Thursday, Good Friday, and Holy Saturday, concluding with Easter Day and Easter Monday. Each day has a special significance. In Italy, both Easter Sunday and Easter Monday [are national holidays](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Italy "Public holidays in Italy"),[[154]](#cite_note-cerimoniale-161) which results in a first and a second Easter Sunday, after which the week continues to a Tuesday.[[154]](#cite_note-cerimoniale-161) Also in the Netherlands, both Easter Sunday and Easter Monday [are national holidays](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_the_Netherlands "Public holidays in the Netherlands"), and like first and second Christmas Day, they are *both* considered Sundays, resulting in a first and a second Easter Sunday, after which the week continues to a Tuesday.[[155]](#cite_note-162)

Good Friday and Saturday as well as Easter Sunday and Monday are traditionally observed [public holidays in Greece](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Greece "Public holidays in Greece"). It is customary for employees of the [public sector](/wiki/Public_sector "Public sector") to receive Easter bonuses as a gift from the state.[[156]](#cite_note-163)

In [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") nations, Easter Sunday is rarely a public holiday, as is the case for celebrations which fall on a Sunday. In the United Kingdom, Good Friday and Easter Monday are [bank holidays](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_the_United_Kingdom "Public holidays in the United Kingdom"), except in Scotland, where only Good Friday is a bank holiday.[[157]](#cite_note-164) In Canada, Easter Monday is a [statutory holiday for federal employees](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Canada#Statutory_holidays_for_federal_employees "Public holidays in Canada"). In the Canadian province of [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"), either Good Friday or Easter Monday are statutory holidays (although most companies give both).[[158]](#cite_note-165) In Australia, Easter is associated with [harvest](/wiki/Harvest "Harvest") time;[[159]](#cite_note-166) Good Friday and Easter Monday are public holidays across all states and territories. The Saturday before Easter is a public holiday in every Australian state except [Tasmania](/wiki/Tasmania "Tasmania") and [Western Australia](/wiki/Western_Australia "Western Australia"), while Easter Sunday itself is a public holiday only in [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"); [Easter Tuesday](/wiki/Easter_Tuesday "Easter Tuesday") is additionally a conditional public holiday in Tasmania, varying upon different industrial [awards](/wiki/Modern_award "Modern award"), and was also a public holiday in [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(state) "Victoria (state)") until 1994.[[160]](#cite_note-167)
In New Zealand, Good Friday and Easter Monday are both state holidays.

In the United States, which is a secular country, Easter is not designated as a federal holiday.[[161]](#cite_note-168) [Easter parades](/wiki/Easter_parade "Easter parade") are held in many American cities, though not sponsored by any government, involving festive strolling processions.[[29]](#cite_note-Duchak2002-32)

### Easter food

Main article: [Easter food](/wiki/Easter_food "Easter food")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c1/Colomba.jpg/250px-Colomba.jpg)](/wiki/File:Colomba.jpg)

[Italian Easter bread](/wiki/Easter_in_Italy "Easter in Italy"), the [Colomba di Pasqua](/wiki/Colomba_di_Pasqua "Colomba di Pasqua"). It is the Easter counterpart of the two well-known [Italian Christmas](/wiki/Christmas_in_Italy "Christmas in Italy") desserts, [panettone](/wiki/Panettone "Panettone") and [pandoro](/wiki/Pandoro "Pandoro")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/Hot_cross_buns_-_fig_and_pecan.jpg/250px-Hot_cross_buns_-_fig_and_pecan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hot_cross_buns_-_fig_and_pecan.jpg)

[Hot cross bun](/wiki/Hot_cross_bun "Hot cross bun")

The holiday of Easter is associated with various [Easter customs](/wiki/Easter_customs "Easter customs") and [foodways](/wiki/Foodways "Foodways") (food traditions that vary regionally). Preparing, coloring, and decorating [Easter eggs](/wiki/Easter_egg "Easter egg") is one such popular tradition. [Lamb](/wiki/Lamb_and_mutton "Lamb and mutton") is eaten in many countries, mirroring the Jewish [Passover](/wiki/Passover "Passover") meal.[[162]](#cite_note-169) Eating lamb at Easter has a religious meaning.[[163]](#cite_note-palaisdurosaire-170) The [Paschal Lamb](/wiki/Lamb_of_God "Lamb of God") of the [New Testament](/wiki/New_Testament "New Testament") is in fact, for Christianity, the son of God Jesus Christ.[[164]](#cite_note-171) The Paschal Lamb, in particular, represents the sacrifice of Jesus Christ for the sins of humanity.[[163]](#cite_note-palaisdurosaire-170) Eating lamb at Easter therefore commemorates the [Death and Resurrection of Jesus](/wiki/Death_and_Resurrection_of_Jesus "Death and Resurrection of Jesus").[[163]](#cite_note-palaisdurosaire-170)

A [hot cross bun](/wiki/Hot_cross_bun "Hot cross bun") is a [spiced bun](/wiki/Spiced_bun "Spiced bun") usually made with fruit, marked with a [cross](/wiki/Christian_cross "Christian cross") on the top, which has been traditionally eaten on [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday") in the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"), [South Africa](/wiki/South_Africa "South Africa"), [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), [India](/wiki/India "India"), [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan"), [Malta](/wiki/Malta "Malta"), [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and the [Commonwealth Caribbean](/wiki/Commonwealth_Caribbean "Commonwealth Caribbean").[[165]](#cite_note-Alexander2017-172)[[166]](#cite_note-caribbean.loopnews.com-173)[[167]](#cite_note-globalvoices.org-174) They are available all year round in some places, including the UK.[[168]](#cite_note-news.bbc.co.uk-175)[[169]](#cite_note-176) The bun marks the end of the Christian season of [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent") and different parts of the hot cross bun have a certain meaning, including the cross representing the [crucifixion of Jesus](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus"), the [spices](/wiki/Spice "Spice") inside signifying the spices used to [embalm](/wiki/Embalming "Embalming") him at his burial and sometimes also [orange peel](/wiki/Orange_peel "Orange peel") to reflect the bitterness of his time on the cross.[[170]](#cite_note-TurnerTaylor1999-177)[[171]](#cite_note-Fakes1994-178) The [Greeks](/wiki/Greeks "Greeks") in the 6th century AD may have marked cakes with a cross.[[172]](#cite_note-179)[[173]](#cite_note-180) In the [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") tradition, the making of buns with a cross on them and consuming them after [breaking the fast](/wiki/Lenten_supper "Lenten supper") on Good Friday, along with "crying about 'Hot cross buns'", is done in order to commemorate the [crucifixion of Jesus](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus").[[174]](#cite_note-Hatton-181) It is hypothesised that the contemporary hot cross bun of Christianity originates from [St Albans Abbey](/wiki/St_Albans_Abbey "St Albans Abbey") in [St Albans](/wiki/St_Albans "St Albans"), England, where in 1361, Brother Thomas Rodcliffe, a 14th-century Christian [monk](/wiki/Monk "Monk"), developed a similar recipe called an 'Alban Bun' and distributed the bun to the poor on [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday").[[175]](#cite_note-182)

For lunch or dinner on [Holy Saturday](/wiki/Holy_Saturday "Holy Saturday"), families in [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") and [Denmark](/wiki/Denmark "Denmark") traditionally feast on a [smörgåsbord](/wiki/Sm%C3%B6rg%C3%A5sbord "Smörgåsbord") of herring, salmon, potatoes, eggs, and other kinds of food. In [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), it is common to eat roasted lamb with potatoes and other vegetables. In Finland, the Lutheran majority enjoys [mämmi](/wiki/M%C3%A4mmi "Mämmi") as another traditional Easter treat, while the Orthodox minority's traditions include eating pasha (also spelled [paskha](/wiki/Paskha_(meal) "Paskha (meal)")) instead. In [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"), the traditional Easter meal is *mageiritsa*, a hearty stew of chopped lamb liver and wild greens seasoned with egg-and-lemon sauce. Traditionally, Easter eggs, hard-boiled eggs dyed bright red to symbolize the spilt Blood of Christ and the promise of eternal life, are cracked together to celebrate the opening of the Tomb of Christ. Greek foods of the Easter tradition are [Flaouna](/wiki/Flaouna "Flaouna"), [Lazarakia](/wiki/Lazarakia "Lazarakia"), [Koulourakia](/wiki/Koulourakia "Koulourakia"), [Magiritsa](/wiki/Magiritsa "Magiritsa") and [Tsoureki](/wiki/Tsoureki "Tsoureki").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/33/Abbacchio_Pasquale.jpg/250px-Abbacchio_Pasquale.jpg)](/wiki/File:Abbacchio_Pasquale.jpg)

[Abbacchio](/wiki/Abbacchio "Abbacchio"), a [lamb](/wiki/Lamb_and_mutton "Lamb and mutton") preparation from the [Italian Easter tradition](/wiki/Easter_in_Italy "Easter in Italy")

Traditional Italian dishes for the Easter period are [abbacchio](/wiki/Abbacchio "Abbacchio"), [cappello del prete](/wiki/Cappello_del_prete "Cappello del prete"), [casatiello](/wiki/Casatiello "Casatiello"), [Colomba di Pasqua](/wiki/Colomba_di_Pasqua "Colomba di Pasqua"), [pastiera](/wiki/Pastiera "Pastiera"), [penia](/wiki/Penia_(bread) "Penia (bread)"), [pizza di Pasqua](/wiki/Pizza_di_Pasqua "Pizza di Pasqua") and [pizzelle](/wiki/Pizzelle "Pizzelle"). Abbacchio is an [Italian](/wiki/Italian_cuisine "Italian cuisine") preparation of [lamb](/wiki/Lamb_and_mutton "Lamb and mutton") typical of the [Roman cuisine](/wiki/Roman_cuisine "Roman cuisine").[[176]](#cite_note-abbacchioromanoigp-prodotto-183)[[177]](#cite_note-Treccani-184) It is a product protected by the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") with the [PGI mark](/wiki/Geographical_indications_and_traditional_specialities_in_the_European_Union#Protected_geographical_indication "Geographical indications and traditional specialities in the European Union").[[178]](#cite_note-PGI-185) In Italy at Easter, abbacchio is cooked in different ways, with recipes that vary from region to region.[[179]](#cite_note-lacucinaitaliana2-186) In [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") it is roasted, in [Apulia](/wiki/Apulia "Apulia") in the oven, in [Naples](/wiki/Naples "Naples") it is cooked with peas and eggs, in [Sardinia](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia") it is cooked in the oven with potatoes, artichokes and myrtle and in [Tuscany](/wiki/Tuscany "Tuscany") it is cooked in [cacciatore](/wiki/Cacciatore "Cacciatore") style.[[179]](#cite_note-lacucinaitaliana2-186) Other local preparations include frying and stewing.[[179]](#cite_note-lacucinaitaliana2-186) [Colomba di Pasqua](/wiki/Colomba_di_Pasqua "Colomba di Pasqua") (English: "Easter Dove") is an [Italian](/wiki/Italian_cuisine "Italian cuisine") traditional [Easter bread](/wiki/Easter_bread "Easter bread"), the Easter counterpart of the two well-known [Italian Christmas](/wiki/Christmas_in_Italy "Christmas in Italy") desserts, [panettone](/wiki/Panettone "Panettone") and [pandoro](/wiki/Pandoro "Pandoro").

[Capirotada](/wiki/Capirotada "Capirotada") or Capilotade, also known as Capirotada de vigilia, is a [traditional](/wiki/Traditional_food "Traditional food") [Mexican food](/wiki/Mexican_food "Mexican food") similar to a [bread pudding](/wiki/Bread_pudding "Bread pudding") that is usually eaten during the [Lenten](/wiki/Lent "Lent") period. It is one of the dishes served on [Good Friday](/wiki/Good_Friday "Good Friday"). Despite originally being consumed before Lent, *capirotada* is now consumed *during* Lent, especially during [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week") and on Good Friday.[[180]](#cite_note-187) Recently, it has been given a spiritual meaning in relation to the passion of Christ and the Lenten season, thus, for many people, the bread represents the Body of Christ, the syrup is his blood, the cloves are the nails of the cross, and the whole cinnamon sticks are the wood of the cross.[[181]](#cite_note-188) The melted cheese stands for the [Holy Shroud](/wiki/Holy_Shroud "Holy Shroud").[[182]](#cite_note-189)

The [Easter mona](/wiki/Easter_mona "Easter mona") is a Spanish kind of [cake](/wiki/Cake "Cake") that is especially eaten on Easter Sunday or [Easter Monday](/wiki/Easter_Monday "Easter Monday") in the [Spanish](/wiki/Spain "Spain") regions of [Catalonia](/wiki/Catalonia "Catalonia"), [Valencia](/wiki/Land_of_Valencia "Land of Valencia") and [Murcia](/wiki/Region_of_Murcia "Region of Murcia").[[183]](#cite_note-190) In other Spanish regions, these Easter cakes are common with variations in the recipe and name. According to the writing of [Joan Amades](/wiki/Joan_Amades "Joan Amades"), mentions of the *mona* date back to the 15th century,[[184]](#cite_note-191) though in the Joan Lacavalleria's 1696 dictionary, *Gazophylacium Catalano-Latinum*, *mona* still has a purely [zoological](/wiki/Zoology "Zoology") definition (meaning female monkey). The 1783 edition of the dictionary of the [Royal Spanish Academy](/wiki/Royal_Spanish_Academy "Royal Spanish Academy") has the following definition: "Catalonia, Valencia and Murcia. Cake baked with eggs in their shell at Easter, known in other parts of the Iberian Peninsula as Hornazo".[[185]](#cite_note-192)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e5/Paskha2.jpg/250px-Paskha2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Paskha2.jpg)

Two [paskhas](/wiki/Paskha "Paskha") with candles (with a [kulich](/wiki/Kulich_(bread) "Kulich (bread)") and [Easter eggs](/wiki/Easter_egg "Easter egg") in the background)

[Paskha](/wiki/Paskha "Paskha") (also spelled *pascha*, or *pasha*) is a Slavic festive dish made in [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") countries which consists of food that is forbidden during the [fast](/wiki/Fasting "Fasting") of [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent"). It is made during [Holy Week](/wiki/Holy_Week "Holy Week") and then brought to Church on [Great Saturday](/wiki/Great_Saturday "Great Saturday") to be blessed after the [Paschal Vigil](/wiki/Paschal_Vigil "Paschal Vigil"). The name of the dish comes from Pascha, the Eastern Orthodox celebration of Easter. Besides [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"), etc. Pasha is also often served in [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"). Cheese paskha is a traditional Easter dish made from [tvorog](/wiki/Tvorog "Tvorog") (like [cottage cheese](/wiki/Cottage_cheese "Cottage cheese"), [Russian](/wiki/Russian_language "Russian language"): творог, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Russian "Romanization of Russian"): *tvorog*),[[186]](#cite_note-193) which is white, symbolizing the purity of Christ, the [Paschal Lamb](/wiki/Lamb_of_God "Lamb of God"), and the joy of the [Resurrection](/wiki/Resurrection "Resurrection"). It is formed in a mold, traditionally in the shape of a truncated [pyramid](/wiki/Pyramid "Pyramid") which symbolizes the first [Passover](/wiki/Passover "Passover") in Egypt, a nod to Christianity's early Jewish beginnings and a reminder that the [Last Supper](/wiki/Last_Supper "Last Supper") of [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus "Jesus") was a [Passover Seder](/wiki/Passover_Seder "Passover Seder"). Others believe the pyramid is a symbol of the [Trinity](/wiki/Trinity "Trinity"), the [Church](/wiki/Christian_Church "Christian Church"), or the [Tomb of Christ](/wiki/Holy_Sepulchre "Holy Sepulchre"). It is usually served as an accompaniment to rich [Easter breads](/wiki/Easter_bread "Easter bread") called [paska](/wiki/Paska_(bread) "Paska (bread)") in Ukraine and [kulich](/wiki/Kulich_(bread) "Kulich (bread)") in Russia (where the "paskha" name is also used in the Southern regions).[[187]](#cite_note-194) The Easter foods; bread and cheese paska are very rich and made of many dairy items given up during [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent"). They are brought to church on Easter to be blessed by the priest.

### Easter eggs

Main article: [Easter egg](/wiki/Easter_egg "Easter egg")

#### Traditional customs

The egg is an ancient symbol of new life and rebirth.[[188]](#cite_note-195) In Christianity it became associated with Jesus's crucifixion and resurrection.[[189]](#cite_note-196) The custom of the Easter egg originated in the early Christian community of [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), who stained eggs red in memory of the [blood of Christ](/wiki/Blood_of_Christ "Blood of Christ"), shed at his crucifixion.[[190]](#cite_note-SiemaszkiewiczDeyrup2013-197)[[191]](#cite_note-198) As such, for Christians, the Easter egg is a symbol of the [empty tomb](/wiki/Empty_tomb "Empty tomb").[[23]](#cite_note-tomb1-26)[[24]](#cite_note-tomb2-27) The oldest tradition is to use dyed [chicken eggs](/wiki/Chicken_egg "Chicken egg").

In the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") Easter eggs are blessed by a priest[[192]](#cite_note-GBN-199) both in families' baskets together with other foods forbidden during [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent") and alone for distribution or in church or elsewhere.

* [![Traditional red Easter eggs for blessing by a priest](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Pasxalina_abga.jpg/330px-Pasxalina_abga.jpg)](/wiki/File:Pasxalina_abga.jpg "Traditional red Easter eggs for blessing by a priest")

  Traditional red Easter eggs for blessing by a priest
* [![A priest blessing baskets with Easter eggs and other foods forbidden during Great Lent](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/2004_Velikden_Pascha_Gorazd_Andrej_Timkovic_Presov_monastyr.jpg/330px-2004_Velikden_Pascha_Gorazd_Andrej_Timkovic_Presov_monastyr.jpg)](/wiki/File:2004_Velikden_Pascha_Gorazd_Andrej_Timkovic_Presov_monastyr.jpg "A priest blessing baskets with Easter eggs and other foods forbidden during Great Lent")

  A priest blessing baskets with Easter eggs and other foods forbidden during [Great Lent](/wiki/Great_Lent "Great Lent")
* [![A priest distributing blessed Easter eggs after blessing the Soyuz rocket](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Expedition_51_Soyuz_Blessing_%28NHQ201704190004%29.jpg/330px-Expedition_51_Soyuz_Blessing_%28NHQ201704190004%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Expedition_51_Soyuz_Blessing_(NHQ201704190004).jpg "A priest distributing blessed Easter eggs after blessing the Soyuz rocket")

  A priest distributing blessed Easter eggs after blessing the Soyuz rocket

Easter eggs are a widely popular symbol of new life among the Eastern Orthodox but also in folk traditions in [Slavic](/wiki/Slavic_people "Slavic people") countries and elsewhere. A batik-like decorating process known as [pisanka](/wiki/Pisanka_(Polish) "Pisanka (Polish)") produces intricate, brilliantly colored eggs. The celebrated [House of Fabergé](/wiki/House_of_Faberg%C3%A9 "House of Fabergé") workshops created [exquisite jewelled Easter eggs](/wiki/Faberg%C3%A9_egg "Fabergé egg") for the Russian Imperial family from 1885 to 1916.[[193]](#cite_note-200)

#### Modern customs

A modern custom in the [Western world](/wiki/Western_world "Western world") is to substitute decorated chocolate, or plastic eggs filled with candy such as [jellybeans](/wiki/Jellybeans "Jellybeans"); as many people give up candy (sweets) as their [Lenten sacrifice](/wiki/Lenten_sacrifice "Lenten sacrifice"), individuals indulge in them at Easter after having abstained during the preceding forty days of [Lent](/wiki/Lent "Lent").[[194]](#cite_note-Shoda2014-201)

* [![Easter eggs, a symbol of the empty tomb, are a popular cultural symbol of Easter.[22]](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Easter_eggs_-_straw_decoration.jpg/330px-Easter_eggs_-_straw_decoration.jpg)](/wiki/File:Easter_eggs_-_straw_decoration.jpg "Easter eggs, a symbol of the empty tomb, are a popular cultural symbol of Easter.[22]")

  Easter eggs, a symbol of the [empty tomb](/wiki/Empty_tomb "Empty tomb"), are a popular cultural symbol of Easter.[[22]](#cite_note-Jordan2000-25)
* [![Marshmallow rabbits, candy eggs and other treats in an Easter basket](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Candy_eggs_in_an_Easter_basket.JPG/330px-Candy_eggs_in_an_Easter_basket.JPG)](/wiki/File:Candy_eggs_in_an_Easter_basket.JPG "Marshmallow rabbits, candy eggs and other treats in an Easter basket")

  Marshmallow rabbits, candy eggs and other treats in an Easter basket
* [![An Easter egg decorated with the Easter Bunny](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e2/Easter-egg-3195.jpg/330px-Easter-egg-3195.jpg)](/wiki/File:Easter-egg-3195.jpg "An Easter egg decorated with the Easter Bunny")

  An Easter egg decorated with the [Easter Bunny](/wiki/Easter_Bunny "Easter Bunny")

Manufacturing their first Easter egg in 1875, British chocolate company [Cadbury](/wiki/Cadbury "Cadbury") sponsors the annual [egg hunt](/wiki/Egg_hunt "Egg hunt") which takes place in over 250 [National Trust](/wiki/National_Trust "National Trust") locations in the United Kingdom.[[195]](#cite_note-202)[[196]](#cite_note-203) On Easter Monday, the President of the United States holds an annual [Easter egg roll](/wiki/Easter_egg_roll "Easter egg roll") on the [White House](/wiki/White_House "White House") lawn for young children.[[197]](#cite_note-204)

##### Easter Bunny

Main article: [Easter Bunny](/wiki/Easter_Bunny "Easter Bunny")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Bunny_Wabbits_%2826218859322%29.jpg/250px-Bunny_Wabbits_%2826218859322%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bunny_Wabbits_(26218859322).jpg)

Inflatable [Easter Bunny](/wiki/Easter_Bunny "Easter Bunny") in front of [San Francisco City Hall](/wiki/San_Francisco_City_Hall "San Francisco City Hall")

In some traditions, the children put out their empty baskets for the Easter Bunny to fill while they sleep. They wake to find their baskets filled with candy eggs and other treats.[[198]](#cite_note-Anderson2017-205)[[30]](#cite_note-Sifferlin2015-33) A custom originating in Germany,[[198]](#cite_note-Anderson2017-205) the Easter Bunny is a popular legendary [anthropomorphic](/wiki/Anthropomorphism "Anthropomorphism") Easter gift-giving character analogous to [Santa Claus](/wiki/Santa_Claus "Santa Claus") in American culture. Many children around the world follow the tradition of [coloring hard-boiled eggs](/wiki/Easter_egg "Easter egg") and giving baskets of candy.[[30]](#cite_note-Sifferlin2015-33) Historically, foxes, cranes and storks were also sometimes named as the mystical creatures.[[198]](#cite_note-Anderson2017-205) Since the [rabbit is a pest](/wiki/Rabbits_in_Australia "Rabbits in Australia") in Australia, the [Easter Bilby](/wiki/Easter_Bilby "Easter Bilby") is available as an alternative.[[199]](#cite_note-206)

2. **[^](#cite_ref-8)** Derived from [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"). [Imperial Aramaic](/wiki/Imperial_Aramaic_language "Imperial Aramaic language"): פַּסְחָא, romanized: *pasḥā*; [Ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_language "Ancient Greek language"): πάσχα, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Ancient_Greek "Romanization of Ancient Greek"): *páskha*. In the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church"), the Greek word *Pascha* is used for the celebration; in English, the analogous word is *Pasch*.[[5]](#cite_note-Ferguson2009-6)[[6]](#cite_note-Etymology-7)
3. **[^](#cite_ref-11)** The term "Resurrection Sunday" is used particularly by Christian communities in the [Middle East](/wiki/Middle_East "Middle East").[[7]](#cite_note-GammanBindon2014-9)[[8]](#cite_note-BodaSmith2006-10)
4. **[^](#cite_ref-37)** Old English pronunciation: [[ˈæːɑstre, ˈeːostre]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Old_English "Help:IPA/Old English").
5. **[^](#cite_ref-71)** Eusebius reports that Dionysius, Bishop of Alexandria, proposed an 8-year Easter cycle, and quotes a letter from Anatolius, Bishop of Laodicea, that refers to a 19-year cycle.[[65]](#cite_note-69) An 8-year cycle has been found inscribed on a statue unearthed in Rome in the 17th century, and since dated to the 3rd century.[[66]](#cite_note-70)
